mechanism: Proposed mechanism: Seed extracts contain escin/aescin (a well-characterized saponin used as an alternate name for horse chestnut products). LiverTox states the mechanism of idiosyncratic liver injury (rare) is unknown.
activeCompounds: Aescin/escin is a well-characterized natural product associated with horse chestnut extracts (also used as an alternate name for products).
contraindications: Individuals with bleeding disorders or taking anticoagulants should avoid use
May interfere with blood sugar regulation; caution in diabetics
May cause mild nausea or abdominal upset
Pregnant or breastfeeding individuals should consult healthcare provider before use
safetyNotes: NCCIH: raw plant parts unsafe orally; standardized extracts likely safe short-term; possible side effects include dizziness, digestive upsets, headache, itching; pregnancy/lactation safety unknown. LiverTox: liver injury rare and typically mild/self-limited with no known cases of acute liver failure/death from horse chestnut.
description: A tree native to parts of southeastern Europe; seeds resemble sweet chestnuts but taste bitter; seed extracts are the common supplement form.
dosage: No direct dosage data. Contextual inference: Venotonic; anti; inflammatory; edema relief. Herbs often act via neurotransmitter modulation; antioxidant effects; or enzyme inhibition.. legalStatus: Approved in EU for venous insufficiency; regulated
preparation: Standardized horse chestnut seed extracts (with toxic component removed) used orally; raw plant material is unsafe.
region: POWO lists native range as Central Balkan Peninsula to Türkiye.
therapeuticUses: Management of chronic venous insufficiency and varicose veins
Treatment of hemorrhoids and venous disorders
sideEffects: high doses can be toxic due to aescin
summary: Horse chestnut seed extract is promoted for chronic venous insufficiency (CVI). NCCIH cites a systematic review suggesting symptom improvement in CVI (with calls for more rigorous trials). Raw seeds/bark/flowers/leaves are unsafe orally due to a toxic component; standardized extracts with the toxic component removed are likely safe for short-term use (studied up to 12 weeks). LiverTox reports rare, generally mild and self-limited liver injury cases attributed to horse chestnut.
